1.exposition: Having announced a better covenant (8:6), the writer now walks the reader through the old sanctuary it surpasses — not in nostalgia, but to gather evidence. He inventories the tent: the outer Holy Place with its lampstand, table, and bread of the Presence; behind the second veil the Most Holy Place with the golden altar of incense and the gold-cased ark, holding the jar of manna, Aaron's budded rod, and the tablets, crowned by the cherubim of glory over the mercy seat (vv.2–5). Then the telling detail of access: priests enter the first room daily, the high priest the second only yearly, and never without blood. From that graded, guarded approach he draws his verdict — the whole apparatus was a parable, its gifts unable to perfect the worshipper's conscience. The old sanctuary's limitation was built into its architecture.
1.original_language: The two rooms are named exactly: the outer hagia ("the Holy Place," v.2) and the inner hagia hagiōn ("the Holy of Holies," v.3). The old covenant had its dikaiōmata latreias ("regulations of worship") and a kosmikon sanctuary — one belonging to this world, material and earthly. The pivot comes at v.9, where the whole system is a parabolē ("symbol, parable") that cannot make the worshipper perfect as to the syneidēsis ("conscience") — the inner moral awareness the letter will press again in v.14. It was, the writer says, provisional "until the time of reformation" (diorthōsis, a setting-right).
1.historical_context: The furnishings correspond to Exodus: the lampstand (Exod 25:31–40), the table of the Presence (Exod 25:23–30), the ark (Exod 25:10–22). One detail differs: placing the golden altar of incense within the Most Holy Place (v.4) does not match Exod 30:6, which sets it in the Holy Place — the writer likely reflects its Day of Atonement function, when the incense was carried within the veil (Lev 16:12–13). The yearly rite is Yom Kippur (Leviticus 16); the Mishnah tractate Yoma records the high priest sequestered a week beforehand and kept awake through the night to guard his purity. The "sins of ignorance" (agnoēmata, v.7) were the inadvertent sins the system covered; deliberate, high-handed sin fell outside its scope.
1.christ: The decisive interpreter of the tent is the Holy Spirit, who "was signifying" (dēloō, v.8) by its very design that the way into the presence stood closed while the first tent stood. The architecture itself was a divine "not yet." That "not yet" became "now" when the temple veil tore at the death of Jesus (Matt 27:51) — the same Spirit who designed the curtain designing its rending, opening what the structure had barred.

verses.note: The first covenant "had also ordinances of divine service, and a worldly sanctuary." grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson weigh the terms: dikaiōmata latreias ("prescribed regulations of worship") and a kosmikon — an earthly, material sanctuary of this world. reformed Calvin sees an elaborate, God-given system, every rite appointed — genuine worship, yet belonging to the visible order the writer is about to relativize.

The outer room: "a tabernacle made; the first, wherein was the candlestick, and the table, and the shewbread; which is called the sanctuary." grammatical-historical Barnes notes the golden lampstand that gave light and the table bearing the twelve loaves set before the Lord — the furniture of the Holy Place (hagia), the room the priests entered daily.

"And after the second veil, the tabernacle which is called the Holiest of all." grammatical-historical Robertson marks the inner curtain — the first being the entrance screen — shutting off the Holy of Holies (hagia hagiōn), the boundary none but the high priest might cross, the type of heaven itself.

Within: "the golden censer, and the ark of the covenant overlaid round about with gold," holding the golden pot of manna, Aaron's rod that budded, and the tables of the covenant — relics of God's provision, his choice of the priesthood (Num 17:8), and his law. grammatical-historical Ellicott and Barnes raise the old difficulty of the "golden censer": whether an incense vessel kept within, or the incense altar (Exod 30:6) associated with the inner room by its Day-of-Atonement use.

"And over it the cherubims of glory shadowing the mercyseat." reformed Calvin ties the cherubim to the visible glory that dwelt above the hilastērion, the golden lid where the atoning blood was sprinkled. grammatical-historical Barnes notes the writer's studied restraint — "of which we cannot now speak particularly" — he forbears to expound each item, hastening to his point about the two rooms.

"The priests went always into the first tabernacle, accomplishing the service of God." puritan Matthew Henry marks the constant round: day by day the ordinary priests entered the Holy Place to trim the lamps, burn incense, and set out the bread — the perpetual, never-finished ministry of the outer room.

"But into the second went the high priest alone once every year, not without blood." grammatical-historical Robertson presses the contrast: one man, one day, and never without blood, offered "for himself, and for the errors of the people" (agnoēmata, sins of inadvertence). The very qualification exposes the weakness — a priest so imperfect he must atone first for his own sin.

"The Holy Ghost this signifying, that the way into the holiest of all was not yet made manifest." grammatical-historical Robertson notes the Spirit designed the tent to teach: its barred inner room was a sign. puritan Henry adds the duration — as long as the first tabernacle was yet standing, the way stood closed; only when the old order gave way in Christ would it open.

The system was "a figure for the time then present" whose gifts and sacrifices "could not make him that did the service perfect, as pertaining to the conscience." reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical Barnes name the crucial deficiency: the old offerings reached the flesh, restoring ceremonial standing, but never the conscience (syneidēsis) — they managed sin's stain without quieting the guilt that troubles the soul.

It "stood only in meats and drinks, and divers washings, and carnal ordinances, imposed on them until the time of reformation." grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson mark the outward, temporary character: rules about food, drink, and ceremonial cleansings — fleshly regulations laid on as a burden with a built-in expiry, binding only until the time of reformation (diorthōsis), the new order in Christ.

witnesses.point: The old worship was a real, God-given system, but earthly and outward — its gifts could purify the flesh, never the conscience.
The elaborate sanctuary and its ordinances were divinely appointed shadows, provisional until the reality came.
The daily service of the priests and the yearly, guarded entrance of the high priest displayed a way to God still closed while the old order stood.

external.name: Harold W. Attridge
external.point: The tabernacle inventory is stylized and selective, not antiquarian description; its purpose is to exhibit the barrier the structure imposed and the restriction the Spirit meant it to teach.

1.application: The old sanctuary preached by its very locks: a God near enough to dwell among his people, yet guarded behind a veil none could pass. Its lesson was a holy dissatisfaction — worship that could cleanse the hands but not quiet the conscience, teaching Israel to long for a better way in.

11.exposition: Against that backdrop the great "But Christ" arrives. He has appeared — high priest of the good things now come — and passes through a greater and more perfect tabernacle, not hand-made, not of this creation. He enters the true sanctuary not with the blood of goats and calves but with his own, and not yearly but once for all, securing not a year's reprieve but eternal redemption. Then the argument from lesser to greater: if a bull's blood and a heifer's ashes could restore ceremonial purity, how much more will the blood of Christ — who through the eternal Spirit offered himself unblemished to God — cleanse the conscience itself from dead works. This is why he mediates a new covenant: his death redeems even the transgressions committed under the old, so that the called may receive the promised eternal inheritance.
11.original_language: The heavenly tent is acheiropoiētos ("not made with hands," v.11) — outside the created order altogether. Christ enters ephapax ("once for all," v.12) and obtains aiōnia lytrōsis ("eternal redemption"). The famous crux is dia pneumatos aiōniou ("through the eternal Spirit," v.14): the Holy Spirit, or Christ's own eternal spirit? He offered himself amōmos ("without blemish") to purge the syneidēsis ("conscience"), and so becomes mesitēs ("mediator," v.15) of the new covenant.
11.historical_context: The Yom Kippur blood the writer measures against Christ's was drawn from two animals — a bull for the high priest and his house, a goat for the people (Lev 16:6–16) — and sprinkled seven times on the mercy seat, the most restricted act in the whole cult. The ashes of the red heifer (Numbers 19), mixed with water and sprinkled on those defiled by a corpse, purified the ceremonially unclean; Jewish tradition confessed even Solomon could not fathom the rite whose administrator himself became unclean by it. "Offered himself without spot" (v.14) draws on the requirement that a victim be physically unblemished (tamim).
11.christ: Here the offering shows its trinitarian shape: the Son offers himself, through the eternal Spirit, to the Father (v.14). Where Yom Kippur was a momentary sprinkling, this offering is made in the Spirit's eternal dimension and is therefore permanent — a single entrance with his own blood that makes him mediator of the new covenant (v.15), its benefits reaching even backward to redeem the sins committed under the first.

verses.note: "But Christ being come an high priest of good things to come, by a greater and more perfect tabernacle, not made with hands." grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson mark the turn: the high priest of the realities the old system only foreshadowed, ministering through a heavenly tent. eastern Cyril and puritan Henry weigh "not of this building" — whether heaven itself or Christ's own humanity, the point is a sanctuary not of this creation.

"Neither by the blood of goats and calves, but by his own blood he entered in once into the holy place, having obtained eternal redemption." reformed Calvin presses hapax — once for all, not the high priest's yearly repetition — a single, final entrance. eastern Cyril notes the fruit: not a year's covering but an eternal redemption, complete and everlasting.

The premise of the argument: "if the blood of bulls and of goats, and the ashes of an heifer sprinkling the unclean, sanctifieth to the purifying of the flesh." grammatical-historical Barnes and eastern Cyril grant the old rites a real but external effect — the red heifer's ashes (Num 19) removed the defilement of death, restoring ceremonial standing, but reached only the flesh, not the conscience.

"How much more shall the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without spot to God, purge your conscience from dead works to serve the living God?" The a fortiori crowns the argument; but "the eternal Spirit" divides the witnesses. reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley read it of the Holy Spirit, under whose power Christ offered himself; grammatical-historical Robertson takes it of Christ's own divine, eternal spirit, the higher principle giving the offering infinite worth (Grotius, less persuasively, of "endless life"). All agree the effect: the spotless self-offering cleanses the conscience the old sacrifices could not touch.

"For this cause he is the mediator of the new testament... that they which are called might receive the promise of eternal inheritance." eastern Chrysostom and reformed Calvin note the retrospective reach of his death — it redeems even "the transgressions that were under the first testament," so that his atonement gathers up the sins the old covenant could never truly remove, and secures the promised eternal inheritance.

witnesses.point: Christ entered once, with his own blood, obtaining an eternal redemption — a single, final offering that ends the yearly repetition of the old.
"Through the eternal Spirit" is best read of Christ's own eternal spirit, the divine principle of his being that gave his self-offering infinite value.
The "eternal Spirit" is the Holy Spirit, under whose influence Christ offered himself — his blood cleanses the conscience, freeing the believer to serve the living God.
By one entrance with his own blood Christ secured a redemption not yearly but eternal — complete, and never to be repeated.

external.name: F.F. Bruce
external.point: "Eternal redemption" is a decisive, unrepeatable securing of deliverance; the single entrance of the exalted Christ through the greater tent accomplishes once for all what the annual ritual could only rehearse.

11.application: A conscience purged "from dead works" is set free "to serve the living God" (v.14). This is the difference between religion as transaction — offering to placate an offended deity — and worship as response: the grateful service of one whom God has definitively forgiven at the cost of his Son's blood.

16.exposition: The word "inheritance" opens a brief legal aside. In Greek diathēkē means both "covenant" and "will," and a will takes effect only when the one who made it dies — so a death was required to activate what Christ bequeaths (vv.16–17). Yet the same truth stands in covenant history: the first covenant, too, was not inaugurated without blood. Moses read the law, took the blood of calves and goats with water, scarlet wool, and hyssop, sprinkled book and people, and pronounced, "This is the blood of the covenant" (v.20); the tent and its vessels were sprinkled as well. The rule is nearly universal: under the law almost everything is cleansed with blood, and — the maxim that governs the whole system — without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness.
16.original_language: The whole passage turns on the double sense of diathēkē — "covenant" (the Septuagint's word for Hebrew berit) and "last will and testament" (ordinary Greek legal usage). At vv.16–17 the writer appears to lean on the testamentary meaning: a will is valid only "over the dead," of no force while the maker lives. The section closes on two coined and weighty terms: haimatekchysia ("shedding of blood") and aphesis ("forgiveness, remission") — with the careful qualifier schedon ("almost," v.22) guarding the rule.
16.historical_context: The blood-inauguration of the Sinai covenant (Exod 24:3–8) was the founding narrative of covenant-making through blood: Moses sprinkled the altar, representing God, and the people, binding the two parties. The hyssop and scarlet wool named here recur in the red heifer rite (Num 19:6) and the Passover (Exod 12:22), weaving the blood-bonds into one web. The formula "This is the blood of the covenant" (Exod 24:8) is verbatim what Jesus said over the cup (Matt 26:28). The "almost" of v.22 is deliberate: the Torah allowed non-blood purifications — water or fire (Num 31:23), the poor's flour offering (Lev 5:11), the atonement silver (Exod 30:15–16) — yet the principle held across the cult.
16.christ: Christ is at once the covenant-maker who dies and the mediator who lives to administer what his death secures. As Moses sealed the first covenant in animal blood, so Jesus consciously inaugurated the new in his own, echoing Moses' very words over the cup (Matt 26:28). The death a testament requires is met in his — and because a testator who has died and risen cannot re-die to revoke the will, the covenant he ratified is permanently in force.

verses.note: "For where a testament is, there must also of necessity be the death of the testator." Here the famous crux: grammatical-historical Robertson hears the deliberate wordplay ("we still say The New Testament"), and with the AV and Gill reads diathēkē as a will valid only at the maker's death. grammatical-historical Clarke and reformed Calvin push back, rendering "covenant" throughout — the death being that of the sacrificial victim that ratifies it — holding the legal "will" foreign to the epistle's Jewish argument. Either way the conclusion stands: death was necessary, and so Christ died.

"For a testament is of force after men are dead: otherwise it is of no strength at all while the testator liveth." grammatical-historical Barnes explains that whether a will (valid only at the maker's death) or a covenant (ratified "over the dead" victim), it has no force until death intervenes — noting that "dead" (nekrois) need not mean only dead men but can embrace the slain sacrifices that seal the bond.

"Whereupon neither the first testament was dedicated without blood." reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical Barnes draw the proof from history: even the Sinai covenant was established with blood, so that the principle — death ratifies a covenant — held for the old and therefore applies to the new.

"When Moses had spoken every precept... he took the blood of calves and of goats, with water, and scarlet wool, and hyssop, and sprinkled both the book, and all the people" (Exod 24). grammatical-historical Barnes notes the writer supplies details (water, wool, hyssop, goats) from the fuller priestly tradition; both covenant document and people were consecrated by blood, sealing the bond.

"Saying, This is the blood of the testament which God hath enjoined unto you" (Exod 24:8). grammatical-historical Robertson hears Moses' words strikingly echoed by Christ at the Supper — "This is my blood of the new testament" (Matt 26:28) — the old covenant's blood foreshadowing the new sealed in his own.

"Moreover he sprinkled with blood both the tabernacle, and all the vessels of the ministry." grammatical-historical Ellicott and Barnes note the pervasiveness: not only book and people, but the sanctuary itself and every implement of worship were consecrated by blood — proof of how essential blood was to the whole old order.

"Almost all things are by the law purged with blood; and without shedding of blood is no remission." grammatical-historical Barnes weighs the guarded "almost" (schedon), allowing exceptions; reformed Calvin lifts up the great maxim — no forgiveness without bloodshed, life given up in death the necessary price. eastern Cyril draws the point home: if even the old covenant required blood for pardon, the true remission of sins demanded the shedding of Christ's.

witnesses.point: "Testament" and "testator" mislead; render diathēkē as "covenant" throughout — the death is the sacrificial victim's, slain to ratify the bond, as at Sinai.
The covenant of grace is genuinely a testament, a will bequeathing an inheritance — which becomes effective only through the death of Christ the testator.
Even the first covenant was dedicated with blood; without the shedding of blood there is no remission, which fixed the necessity of Christ's death.

external.name: William L. Lane
external.point: For these two verses the author plays on the everyday sense of diathēkē as a last will, whose provisions become operative only upon the death of the one who drew it up — a legal commonplace pressed into the service of the argument.

16.application: "Without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness" (v.22) is no celebration of violence but a measure of cost. Forgiveness is not cheap sentiment; it was purchased at the price of a life. Grace received lightly has not yet been understood — the weight of the blood is the weight of the mercy.

23.exposition: The conclusion gathers force. If the earthly copies needed cleansing by animal blood, the heavenly realities they shadowed needed better sacrifices. So Christ entered not a hand-made sanctuary but heaven itself, now appearing before God's face on our behalf (v.24). And he entered once — not, like the high priest, year after year with borrowed blood, for that would mean suffering again and again since the world's foundation. Instead, at the climax of the ages he appeared once to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself. The writer seals it with the rhythm of every human life: as we die once and then face judgment, so Christ was offered once to bear the sins of many — and will appear a second time, no longer to deal with sin, but to bring salvation to those who eagerly wait for him.
23.original_language: The earthly things are hypodeigmata ("copies, patterns," v.23) and antitypa ("copies of the true," v.24). The chapter's governing note is the decisive hapax / ephapax ("once for all"). Christ appears epi synteleia tōn aiōnōn ("at the culmination of the ages," v.26) to accomplish the athetēsis ("abolition, putting-away") of sin. At v.28 he is offered "to bear" (anapherō, to carry up and away, echoing Isa 53:12) the sins of many, and will appear to those "eagerly awaiting" him (apekdechomai).
23.historical_context: The contrast with Yom Kippur exposes its structural limit: the high priest brought "blood not his own" (v.25) — a substitute's blood at one remove — into the most restricted space in Israel's cult, and could never stay. Meanwhile the people waited outside; his reappearance from the sanctuary was the sign that atonement had been accepted (Lev 16). "At the culmination of the ages" (v.26) is apocalyptic idiom for the turning-point of history; Christ's promised second appearing answers, in kind, the high priest's emergence to the waiting congregation.
23.christ: The two appearings mirror the Day of Atonement's own shape: the priest enters the inner room and then comes out to the waiting people. Christ has entered heaven itself with his own blood; his return will be no second sacrifice — sin having been dealt with once — but the announcement that the sacrifice has done all it was meant to do (v.28): the fully realized salvation, the redemption of the body, the inheritance secured.

verses.note: "It was therefore necessary that the patterns of things in the heavens should be purified with these; but the heavenly things themselves with better sacrifices." puritan Henry and grammatical-historical Barnes handle the difficulty of "purifying" heaven: not that heaven was defiled, but that the heavenly sanctuary is inaugurated and its way consecrated by a better sacrifice — the blood of Christ.

"Christ is not entered into the holy places made with hands... but into heaven itself, now to appear in the presence of God for us." eastern Chrysostom marks the intercession: Christ presents himself and his finished sacrifice before God's very face on our behalf; his entrance into the true sanctuary is for our sake, not into a mere copy (antitypa) of it.

"Nor yet that he should offer himself often, as the high priest entereth into the holy place every year with blood of others." reformed Calvin notes the double contrast — repeated versus once, others' blood versus his own: the very repetition of the Levitical entrance proved its insufficiency, whereas Christ's single offering of his own blood needs no renewal.

"For then must he often have suffered since the foundation of the world: but now once in the end of the world hath he appeared to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself." grammatical-historical Barnes and eastern Cyril press the logic: since his offering was his death, repetition would demand repeated suffering — an absurdity. Instead, hapax, at the consummation of the ages, he appeared to abolish sin (athetēsis) by one self-sacrifice.

"As it is appointed unto men once to die, but after this the judgment." grammatical-historical Robertson notes the fixed, divine appointment — every person dies a single time, and after death comes judgment; the emphatic "once" sets up the parallel with Christ's single death, against any notion of repeated dyings or a second probation.

"So Christ was once offered to bear the sins of many; and unto them that look for him shall he appear the second time without sin unto salvation." eastern Cyril draws the Day-of-Atonement picture: as the high priest, having offered, came out to bless the waiting people, so Christ will appear again — not now to deal with sin (that being finished) but to bring full salvation to those who eagerly await him, having borne the sins of many (Isa 53:12).

witnesses.point: Christ entered heaven itself, not a copy, to appear before God on our behalf — and will emerge again, as the high priest did, to the people awaiting him.
The yearly repetition of the old sacrifices proved their insufficiency; Christ's single offering of his own blood is final and needs no renewal.
Repeated offering would require repeated suffering; instead, once at the end of the ages, Christ put away sin by the one sacrifice of himself.

external.name: Peter T. O'Brien
external.point: The sacrifice is emphatically eschatological — offered once at the turning-point of the ages, it can neither be repeated nor supplemented; the second appearing brings the consummated salvation that the completed offering already guarantees.

23.application: The community lives in the interval between Christ's two appearings — the completed sacrifice behind, the completed salvation ahead. Its posture is therefore not anxious religious effort to secure what is still lacking, but the alert, hopeful waiting of those who "eagerly await him" (v.28). There is no more sacrifice to add; there is only the sufficiency of his to trust, and his return to expect.

_meta.title: Redemption by His Own Blood
reflection: The blood of Christ "purges your conscience from dead works to serve the living God" (9:14). Where are you still serving God as a debtor managing guilt, rather than as one whose conscience has already been cleansed?
"Without shedding of blood is no remission" (9:22). How does reckoning with the cost of your forgiveness change the way you receive it — and the way you extend it to others?

Christ "shall appear the second time without sin unto salvation" to those who eagerly await him (9:28). What would it look like to live now, between his appearings, with alert hope instead of anxious effort?
